title: SEGMENT 5 — First-generation adenoviral vectors: E1/E3 deletions and the original gene therapy hope

lines.text: SEGMENT 5 — First-generation adenoviral vectors: E1/E3 deletions and the original gene therapy hope
Let's go back to the late 1980s and early 1990s.
The first generation of adenoviral vectors had just been developed, and the entire gene therapy community was electrified.
Here was a virus that could infect almost any human cell.
It could be grown to enormous titers — billions of particles per milliliter, easily.
It could deliver large payloads.
And it had a key structural advantage over the retroviruses that had been the early gene therapy vectors of choice — adenovirus doesn't integrate.
It stays as an episome in the nucleus.
So there was no insertional mutagenesis risk, no chance of accidentally activating an oncogene by inserting the vector next to it.
Adenovirus seemed almost too good to be true.
And, well — it almost was.
Here's the recipe for a first-generation adenoviral vector.
You start with the genome of human adenovirus type 5 — the most-studied human adenovirus, the one we have the most molecular tools for.
You delete the E1 region — that master-switch region that activates all the other viral genes.
With E1 gone, the virus can't replicate.
It can still infect, still deliver its DNA to the nucleus, still express genes that you put in under their own promoters.
But it can't make new virus particles.
You've crippled it.
But now you have a problem: how do you produce the vector in the first place?
If the virus can't replicate, you can't grow it.
The solution was elegant.

A cell line called HEK293 — human embryonic kidney 293 — had been created in the 1970s by Frank Graham, and HEK293 cells happen to have integrated, into their own genome, a piece of the adenoviral genome containing the E1 region.
So HEK293 cells stably express E1 proteins from their own chromosome.
Which means: if you put an E1-deleted adenoviral vector into HEK293 cells, the cells supply E1 in trans, the vector can replicate inside those cells, and you can harvest packaged vector particles.
But if you put that same vector into any other human cell — a patient's liver cell, say — there's no E1 anywhere, and the vector just sits there as a one-shot delivery vehicle.
HEK293 cells are still, today, the workhorse producer line for first-generation adenoviral vectors.
Every COVID-era adenoviral vaccine was produced in either HEK293 cells or a closely related line.
The pairing of E1-deleted vector and E1-complementing cell line is the central engineering trick that makes the whole platform possible.
Now, the typical first-generation vector also has the E3 region deleted.
Recall from Segment 3 that E3 encodes proteins that help the wild virus evade host immunity — particularly cytotoxic T cells.
The wild virus needs E3 to persist in an infected animal.
But for vector production in tissue culture, E3 is dispensable.
So we delete it, and we gain about 3.5 kb of extra cargo space.
Together, deleting E1 and E3 frees up about 8 kb of cargo capacity in a first-generation vector.
You can stuff in an 8 kb expression cassette — promoter, gene of interest, polyA signal, maybe a marker — and the vector will deliver and express it in patient cells.
In the early 1990s, this design was used in dozens of preclinical and early clinical studies.
Cystic fibrosis was a major target — the idea was to deliver the CFTR gene to lung epithelial cells via inhaled adenoviral vector.
Familial hypercholesterolemia was another target.
Various cancers were targeted, by delivering tumor suppressor genes or genes that made cancer cells sensitive to particular drugs.

And the OTC deficiency trial, which we'll discuss in detail in the next segment, was an attempt to deliver a functional copy of the ornithine transcarbamylase gene to liver cells via direct hepatic artery infusion.
But there was a problem nobody had quite reckoned with, and in retrospect it should have been obvious.
First-generation vectors have E1 deleted, yes, but they still have all the other viral genes — E2, E4, the late genes.
Those genes are not expressed when E1 is missing, in theory.
But in practice, in patient cells, those genes get expressed at low levels.
There's leaky transcription.
There are stray promoter activities.
There are pieces of viral protein wandering around in the cell.
And when patient T cells encounter a cell expressing fragments of viral protein, they recognize that cell as infected and they kill it.

So the very cells you were trying to treat — the liver cells expressing the corrective gene — were being slowly destroyed by the patient's own T cells, because they were also presenting bits of viral protein from the leaky residual viral genes.
The therapeutic effect lasted maybe a few weeks at best, then faded as the transduced cells were killed off.
And the patient was left with a strong immune response against adenovirus and against the corrective protein, making redosing essentially impossible.
This is what the analogy I gave you in Segment 1 was pointing at.
In a first-generation vector, the drug works, but the leftover viral genes still get expressed and the immune system mounts a response.
The truck arrived.
The cargo was delivered.
But the truck's engine kept rumbling, and the neighborhood watch arrested the homeowner who took delivery of the package.
The therapy lasted briefly and then was extinguished.

The field's response to this realization, by the mid-1990s, was to start designing second-generation and third-generation vectors with deeper and deeper deletions, trying to eliminate every last viral gene that could trigger T cell killing.
We'll get to those in Segments 7 and 8.
But before we do, we need to sit with the moment when the first-generation problem became horrifyingly real.
We need to talk about Jesse Gelsinger.
